steps.title: Understand the researchers' conclusion about latitude
steps.content: The researchers concluded that the prevalence of the trait (the proportion of species that have it) is mediated by absolute latitude — meaning the trait should be more common in some latitude zones than others.

steps.title: Find evidence showing how prevalence varies with latitude
steps.content: Choice B provides the key evidence: 34% of species at 0°–30° latitude have the trait compared to 60% at 50°–80° latitude, showing that prevalence increases with latitude. The other options either deny the latitude-dependent pattern, introduce confounding exceptions, or describe distribution without measuring prevalence variation. That matches choice B — B is correct.
